title: Rajesh Kumar Sharma v. Hindustan Technologies Pvt. Ltd.
keyArguments: The employee was terminated following a duly conducted domestic enquiry that adhered to principles of natural justice, including issuance of charge sheet and opportunity to respond.
Misconduct involving misappropriation of company funds amounting to Rs. 3,40,000 was established through documentary evidence and witness testimony.
The petitioner failed to appear before the Enquiry Officer on two scheduled dates without adequate reason, thereby forfeiting his right to cross-examine witnesses.
The Labour Court had erred in setting aside the termination order without properly appreciating the evidence on record.
The employee's claim of victimization due to trade union activities was unsubstantiated and unsupported by any documentary or oral evidence.
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Delhi allowed the writ petition filed by Hindustan Technologies Pvt. Ltd. challenging the Labour Court's award that had reinstated the workman Rajesh Kumar Sharma with back wages. The Court held that the domestic enquiry was conducted fairly and in accordance with principles of natural justice, and that the charge of financial misconduct was proved beyond reasonable doubt. The impugned award of the Labour Court was set aside and the termination of the workman was upheld.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI

W.P.(C) No. 7823 of 2022

JUDGMENT
Date of Decision: 14th March, 2023

HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ANIL KUMAR VERMA

1. This writ petition under Article 226 of the Constitution of India has been filed by the petitioner-employer, Hindustan Technologies Pvt. Ltd., challenging the Award dated 08.09.2021 passed by the Presiding Officer, Labour Court No. IV, Delhi, in ID No. 312/2018, whereby the termination of the respondent-workman, Rajesh Kumar Sharma, was set aside and reinstatement with 50% back wages was directed.

2. Brief facts are that the respondent-workman was employed as a Senior Accounts Executive with the petitioner company since 2009. In the year 2017, during an internal audit, certain irregularities were detected in petty cash disbursements maintained under the supervision of the respondent, involving an alleged misappropriation of Rs. 3,40,000 over a period of 18 months. A charge sheet was issued on 12.04.2017 and a domestic enquiry was initiated. The Enquiry Officer submitted his report on 05.08.2017 finding the charges proved. Thereafter, the respondent was terminated vide order dated 22.09.2017.

3. The respondent raised an industrial dispute which was referred to the Labour Court. The Labour Court, by the impugned Award, held that the enquiry was vitiated on the ground that the workman was not given adequate opportunity to cross-examine witnesses and thus set aside the termination.

4. Learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that the Labour Court committed a manifest error in law by holding that the enquiry was vitiated. It was argued that the respondent was issued notices on two separate dates i.e., 18.06.2017 and 29.06.2017 for cross-examination of witnesses, but he chose not to appear without furnishing any valid reason. The right to cross-examine cannot be exercised unilaterally by the workman after deliberately absenting himself.

5. Per contra, learned counsel for the respondent-workman submitted that the enquiry was conducted in haste and the workman was not given sufficient time to arrange legal representation. It was further submitted that the termination was in fact motivated by the workman's active participation in trade union activities.

6. I have carefully considered the rival submissions and perused the record.

7. On the question of the validity of the domestic enquiry, this Court finds that the Enquiry Officer had duly served notices upon the workman for cross-examination on two occasions. The workman's absence on both dates was neither explained before the Enquiry Officer nor before the Labour Court through credible evidence. It is well settled that principles of natural justice require an opportunity to be given, not that such opportunity must necessarily be availed. Reference is made to the judgment of the Supreme Court in Bharat Forge Co. Ltd. v. Uttam Manohar Nakate, (2005) 2 SCC 489, wherein it was held that an employee who deliberately refuses to participate in an enquiry cannot subsequently challenge it as violative of natural justice.

8. On the question of alleged victimization, no evidence whatsoever was led by the respondent before the Labour Court to establish a nexus between his trade union activities and the initiation of the disciplinary proceedings. The finding of the Labour Court on this aspect is entirely speculative.

9. The charge of misappropriation was established by documentary evidence including vouchers, audit reports, and the testimony of two witnesses from the accounts department, which evidence remained unrebutted.

10. Accordingly, this Court finds that the Labour Court committed an error apparent on the face of the record in setting aside the termination. The impugned Award dated 08.09.2021 is hereby quashed and set aside. The termination order dated 22.09.2017 is upheld. The writ petition is allowed. No order as to costs.
